headlines.headline: Wall Street ends higher as Middle East peace talks lift sentiment
headlines.summary: STORY: U.S. stocks closed higher on Thursday, with the Dow and S&P 500 each adding roughly six-tenths of a percent, and the Nasdaq climbing more than eight-tenths of a percent. All three stock indexes rebounded from an earlier dip as Israel sought talks with Lebanon, explains Eric Diton, president and managing director of The Wealth Alliance. “So the markets initially were down in the morning. Concerns about the Strait of Hormuz being closed. Concerns about Israel and Hezbollah and continuing battles over there. But I think we got some positive news in that Israel announced that it wants to have immediate talks with Lebanon. I don't know how those will end up going, but that was positive. And there's just a lot of talk amongst UAE, the U.S., other allies, that, 'Hey, we've got to get the Strait of Hormuz open.' Let's be clear, it's not open right now. Despite the ceasefire, there's virtually no flow of oil going through the Strait. That's going to have to change. Wall Street is betting that it will. I make that same bet.” Oil prices rose but stayed below $100 a barrel. Stocks on the move Thursday included Amazon, which rose 5.5% after CEO Andy Jassy said AI services at its cloud-computing unit are generating annualized revenue of more than $15 billion. Shares of Constellation Brands jumped 8.5% after the company posted a smaller-than-expected drop in fourth-quarter sales. ::File On the downside, shares of Applied Digital dropped 8% after the data center operator's third-quarter net loss widened from a year earlier. Meanwhile, data from the Commerce Department showed the economy grew at a slower-than-expected pace in the fourth quarter, while the personal expenditures price index showed inflation remains elevated. A separate inflation reading from the Bureau of Labor Statistics is due on Friday.

US stocks end mixed on signs of progress before Trump's Iran deadline
headlines.summary: STORY: Wall Street's main indexes ended mixed, with the Dow dipping almost two-tenths of a percent, and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq moving marginally higher. :: File Stocks pared deeper losses in the last hour of trading, with S&P 500 and Nasdaq edging into positive territory minutes before the closing bell. The reversal came after Pakistan's prime minister said on X that diplomatic efforts for a peaceful settlement of the war in the Middle East were progressing steadily. He urged President Donald Trump to extend his Tuesday deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz for two weeks, and requested that Iran open the Strait for the same timeframe as a gesture of good will. But Matt Stucky, chief portfolio manager for equities at Northwestern Mutual Wealth Management Company, said that a near-term off-ramp could still leave markets in murky waters. "Unfortunately, with this conflict in the Middle East, the theater of this engagement has become more widespread. And if we were to see potentially the U.S. back away from involvement, there's still the lingering concern that markets are likely to have that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz will likely continue to be in a diminished capacity or in a kind of more expensive profile for seeing transit through that strait. So it's a messier de-escalation ramp exit versus what we saw at this point last year with Liberation Day." Among the session's stock moves, UnitedHealth jumped nearly 9.5% after the U.S. government announced on Monday it would raise payments to private insurers offering Medicare Advantage plans to older adults, an increase from the near-flat change proposed earlier. Peer Humana rose about 8% and CVS Health gained more than 6.5%. Shares of Apple dropped more than 2% after a report that the tech giant's long-awaited foldable phone is encountering engineering setbacks. And chipmaker Broadcom climbed more than 6% after signing a long-term deal with Alphabet to develop its AI chips and other components.

headlines.summary: Casey’s General Stores the Iowa-based convenience store chain, will replace medical technology company Hologic in the S&P 500 before trading opens on Thursday, S&P Dow Jones Indices said late Monday. Hologic is being acquired by affiliates of Blackstone and TPG Global in a deal expected to be completed on or about Tuesday. Barron’s reported in October that Blackstone and TPG were in advanced talks to acquire the medical device maker, although the companies declined to confirm the talks.

headlines.headline: Wall St ends higher as investors parse US-Iran negotiations, threats
headlines.summary: STORY: U.S. stocks ended higher on Monday with the Nasdaq leading Wall Street's main indexes with a more than half-a-percent gain. "The entire country can be taken out in one night." President Donald Trump has threatened to escalate the war if Iran fails to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. :: Majid Asgaripour/WANA But investors drew some reassurance from a report that indicated the U.S., Iran and a group of regional mediators continued to discuss terms of a potential ceasefire deal. That helped stocks edge higher, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq gaining for the fourth consecutive session, their longest winning streaks since January. Jeff Schulze, head of economic and market strategy for ClearBridge Investments, said that while volatility will likely continue, so too should stocks' recent upward momentum. "Investors are using the playbook of buying the geopolitical dip. When you go back to 1950, there's been over 20 military conflicts. And if you had bought the day that that conflict began, the S&P 500 has been positive 3% over the next three months and positive almost 6% over the next six months. And with the S&P 500 down almost 5% over the last month, we think that this is probably a good buying opportunity for investors, given the resilience of the U.S. economy.” Stocks on the move Monday included Soleno Therapeutics, which jumped more than 32% after Neurocrine Biosciences agreed to acquire the rare-disease drugmaker for $2.9 billion in cash. Rising bitcoin prices boosted crypto-linked firms, with U.S.-listed shares of Coinbase gaining about 2% and Strategy climbing 6.5%. And shares of memory chipmaker Seagate Technology gained 5.5% after Morgan Stanley added it to its top-pick list.
